source_title: Avicenna — Compendium on the Soul (Intellect/Logic, excerpt)
text_chunk: Now this intellect is either in potency, or in act... As for (A), it is not possible that it should dull from activity or be fatigued; and this may be established by the method of logic... But it is absurd to suppose that the nature of the intellect should be changed by its perceiving things...

source_title: Avicenna — The Canon of Medicine (Definition, excerpt)
text_chunk: Medicine is the science by which we learn the various states of the human body in health, and when not in health...

source_title: Avicenna — Compendium on the Soul (Faculties/Senses, excerpt)
text_chunk: Wherefore all the powers of the animal are either perceiving or motion-promoting... The perceiving power too is either outward (apparent), such as the five senses; or else inward (internal, hidden), such as the picturing, the imaginative, the conjecturing, and the remembering power... (allegory) the five senses, the spies that are sent forth... the remembering power, the closet of state papers.

source_title: Avicenna — Canon of Medicine (Humours & Heat/Cold, summary)
notes: [INFERENCE] Summary/paraphrase; treat as adaptation in strict mode.
text_chunk:
Avicenna describes four humours as by-products of digestion/aliment with distinct qualities:

Blood: forms from aliment of equable temperament.

Bilious humour: an attenuated by-product described as hot (also “sweet/oily/sharp”).

Serous humour: a dense, humid/viscid, cold by-product.

Atrabilious humour: a very dense by-product, deficient in moisture, associated with excess heat (and also linked to cold in a “congelative” excess context).

He also states the formation rule in terms of heat/cold balance: “When the heat is equable, blood forms …” and when heat goes into excess, bilious humour forms; when cold is equable, serous humour forms; when cold is excessive toward congelation, atrabilious humour forms.
